segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with serene static shots of a modern, minimalist interior, showcasing gray walls adorned with decorative elements like a white textured vase holding black foliage, a round mirror, and various geometric wall art pieces in gold and black frames, all set to gentle, upbeat background music. A female narrator greets the audience, introducing "today's DIY projects" focused on crafting with "Dollar Tree items" to create "beautiful home decor items on a budget," mentioning that all items will be linked in the description for shopping convenience. The scene then transitions to a close-up of hands with light pink manicured nails, which the narrator confirms are hers as she states, "We're making a vase." She holds a clear glass vase, identified as a "large vase from the Dollar Tree," and a beige package of 'googly eyes / yeux remuants'. The hands open the package, pouring the googly eyes into a clear glass bowl, with the narrator explaining she's separating the larger pieces for future projects because they won't sit flat on the curved vase. She then uses a black and yellow hot glue gun to apply adhesive to the vase, initially using "hot glue" before switching to "tacky glue" for speed, meticulously attaching the googly eyes one by one to cover the vase in a dense, playful pattern.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a top-down view of a crafting process, where hands with pink nails meticulously decorate a clear glass vase, already transformed into a white textured surface covered in googly eyes. A female voice instructs, "All you have to do is brush the tacky glue on a small area and then apply all your googly eyes." The hands use a small, blue-handled paintbrush to apply white adhesive to the vase's base, then switch to black tweezers to precisely place individual googly eyes. A glass bowl containing a mix of white and black googly eyes is positioned nearby, with some scattered on the white surface. The voice continues, "I'm also using my tweezer to place the pieces... And just make sure that you're not overlapping the pieces," emphasizing the careful, methodical placement to create a dense, uniform pattern. She further advises on edge placement: "And on the edges, you just want to go around and make sure that it's straight first and then you can just fill in the areas." The process is detailed and deliberate, highlighting precision and care. The 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark is faintly visible across the frame. Towards the end, the voice states, "Now here's how it looks. I'm going to take it outside and spray paint it with my Rust-Oleum white gloss," revealing the next step in the DIY project and the product used to achieve the vase's white finish.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a backdrop of calm, instrumental music, hands with pink nails methodically apply Gorilla Wood Glue to three overlapping wooden embroidery hoops, guided by a yellow tape measure. Finally, two additional 12-inch round mirrors are positioned symmetrically on either side, completing the precise, three-mirror wall art design.

The workspace remains clean and well-lit, emphasizing precision and organization, with the text 'Budget Friendly Creations' visible as a watermark throughout the segment. She then instructs that the "next cut will be 8 inches down, and we're cutting two pieces of those." The deliberate and focused actions highlight a step-by-step crafting process, all set to a backdrop of gentle, calm instrumental music.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands with pink nails holding a bottle of Gorilla Wood Glue, as a female voice begins to narrate, stating, "Next, I'm just lining up my pieces." The hands then meticulously arrange light-colored wooden dowels horizontally on a white surface, with the speaker explaining the precise arrangement: "The 36 inches will be in the center and then the 32 inches will be on the sides and so on and so forth to the smallest which is 16 inches." A yellow tape measure is used to ensure accurate 2-inch spacing, as indicated by on-screen text. Following this, the hands use a hot glue gun to apply adhesive along the dowels, with the speaker instructing, "Just hold them very tight and use your hot glue to attach the pieces," emphasizing, "You want them to be very tight together." After pressing the dowels firmly, the speaker notes, "Now I flip them over and I'm just making sure that they're nice and tight." Blue painter’s tape is then applied along the edges to secure the dowels, with the speaker clarifying, "And I'm using my painter's tape to hold them together. And I'll just attach two pieces of my painter's tape." Finally, the speaker states, "Now I'll flip them back on the front," and a paintbrush is used to apply a white adhesive or sealant over the dowel panel, as the speaker concludes, "And I'm using my wood glue to connect the pieces together permanently."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with upbeat, light background music as hands with pink nails meticulously wrap the rim of a clear glass vase with blue painter’s tape, securing it with a rubber band. A grid-patterned paper is then applied to the vase with more blue painter’s tape. A female voice explains, "I'm also placing something on the inside to catch the paint," as the preparation continues. The speaker then states, "Now we have all our items dry and we're going to take them outside and spray paint them," adding, "and I'm using my Rust-Oleum reflective gold spray paint. Just give them two nice coats." The vase is sprayed with Rust-Oleum Metallic Gold spray paint, transforming its upper section into a shiny gold finish. After drying, the speaker confirms, "Now here they are, nice and dry." The hands peel off the tape and paper, revealing the clean, gold-rimmed design. Addressing some residue, the speaker notes, "I have some overspray on the inside. So I'm just using my alcohol to clean off that overspray," as the hands wipe the vase with a cloth. The scene transitions to the hands holding a gold-painted safety cone, which the speaker refers to as "the cone area," explaining, "just to cover it up and make it look neat, I'm going to be using foam board from the Dollar Tree," while a pencil marks the cone. The entire DIY crafting process unfolds against a clean white background, with "Budget Friendly Creations" watermarked throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a pair of hands, featuring light pink manicured nails, meticulously tracing a circular shape on white paper using a yellow pencil, then refining the outline with a silver pen. A calm instrumental background music plays as a female narrator instructs, "just place the cone on top of the foam board and trace it with your pencil and then cut it out with your exacto knife," further advising to "cut it on the inside of your mark, so it will fit inside of the cone." The scene transitions to a gold-painted safety cone, which the hands hold and adjust. The narrator explains, "Now add your foam to the base, and I'm using just my hot glue to add the foam." The hands then use a hot glue gun to apply a continuous bead of hot glue along the inner rim of the cone’s circular opening. A piece of black mesh fabric is laid flat, and the gold-painted cone is placed on top of it. The narrator continues, "And then I'll be covering it up with a piece of dust cloth, just to make it neat, you can use the Dollar Tree felt," and discusses making it heavier by adding "tacky glue on the inside of the cone and sprinkle it with sand." The hands use scissors to carefully cut the black mesh fabric around the base of the cone, trimming it to fit snugly. The final action shows the hands lifting the trimmed cone, now wrapped with the black mesh, as the narrator shifts to a call for engagement, asking viewers to "please give me a thumbs up" and "leaving me a comment" to support her channel. The white surface remains clean and well-lit throughout, with the watermark 'Budget Friendly Creations' visible.

segments.integrated_caption: A female voice narrates a DIY project as hands with pink nails begin by placing a grid-patterned paper on a white surface. The segment concludes with a small metal bracket being attached to the assembled wooden panel with a power drill, preparing it for hanging, as the narrator mentions using "two" of something, before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with gentle, upbeat background music as hands with pink nails methodically place a single wooden dowel horizontally on a white surface, with the faint 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark visible. A female narrator explains, "And I'm laying them in a staggered motion," adding, "I'm placing the largest one first" as she uses a hot glue gun to apply adhesive to the dowel's end. She then presses a second, third, and fourth dowel alongside it, forming a precisely aligned parallel bundle. The hands carefully adjust the dowels for a tight fit. The narrator continues, "Now I'll go ahead and attach my design to my frame," as the hands pick up a longer, flat wooden plank, which she refers to as "my frame," and position it vertically next to the dowel bundle. The hot glue gun is used again to apply adhesive along the plank's edge, and the dowel bundle is pressed firmly onto it, creating a perpendicular joint. The narrator clarifies, "And I'm using my strong adhesive. For my strong adhesive, I'm using Aleene's Mixed Media Adhesive," as the hands use a paintbrush to spread Gorilla Wood Glue evenly along the seam where the dowels meet the plank, ensuring a strong bond. She adds, "And I'm placing the glue about one and a half inch on my frame, and then I'll attach my design," and "I'll do that for both sides," as additional glue is applied to the ends of the dowel bundle, securing the structure with careful alignment and strong adhesion.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with upbeat, light background music as hands with pink nails meticulously arrange light-colored wooden dowels perpendicularly onto a flat wooden plank, securing them with adhesive. A female voice explains, "Now I'll go ahead and use some indoor latex paint to prime my base," as the hands use a blue-handled paintbrush to apply a light purple paint along the length of the plank, partially covering the dowels. After the paint dries, the speaker states, "After that was dry, I took it outside and I spray painted the back matte black, and the front I use my reflective gold spray paint." This is shown as the painted plank is moved outdoors onto a weathered white wooden bench and sprayed with Rust-Oleum Ultra Cover 2X Flat Black spray paint, transforming it to a uniform matte black. Once dry, the speaker notes, "Now we're all dry, this is how it looks." The hands then reassemble the dowels onto the now-black plank, aligning them neatly, and apply a gold metallic paint to the ends of the dowels, creating a striking contrast. The scene shifts to a black circular base, where the speaker announces, "I'm going to go ahead and attach my mirrors," adding, "And I'm using my strong adhesive and my hot glue," as the hands apply white adhesive from a tube with a nozzle along the inner rim, preparing it for attachment. The entire DIY crafting tutorial, featuring the faint 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark, is accompanied by the continuous background music.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a clean white background, subtly marked with the 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark, a methodical crafting process unfolds to the accompaniment of gentle, upbeat background music. A female narrator guides the viewer, beginning by applying adhesive, which she refers to as 'hot glue,' from a tube along the rim of a black circular base. Her pink-nailed hands then attach this base to a gold-painted wooden hoop with a beige fabric backing, a unit she calls her 'first mirror.' She carefully positions this 'mirror' onto a long flat wooden plank, which she identifies as the 'frame,' ensuring precise alignment. This assembly is repeated for a second 'mirror' on the opposite side and a third in the center, with her hands meticulously pressing each unit into place. To prepare the 'wall decoration' for hanging, the wooden 'frame' is flipped over. The narrator explains she's using a 'picture frame hook' (a metal bracket) and her 'hot glue' to initially attach it, before securing it firmly with 'screws' using what she calls her 'screwdriver' (a power drill). The segment concludes with the finished structure, showcasing three evenly spaced circular units along the plank, now fully prepared for mounting.

This assembly is repeated to create a series of three circular units aligned along the wooden plank. The hands carefully position and press each unit into place, ensuring alignment. Subsequently, the wooden plank is flipped over, and a metal bracket is placed and secured with a power drill, creating a mounting point. The entire structure is then shown with three circular units evenly spaced along the plank, now ready for hanging. The process is methodical, emphasizing precision in alignment and secure attachment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of hands with light pink manicured nails carefully removing a beige fabric backing from a circular gold-framed mirror, revealing a black adhesive, as a female voice instructs to "remove your paper and clean your mirrors with alcohol." The scene transitions to a completed geometric wall art piece — a tall, vertical arrangement of three circular gold-framed mirrors with spiky gold accents — mounted on a gray wall, with the female voice commenting, "Here's how this project turned out, guys. I hope you like this one." The camera pans up the reflective wall art before shifting to a crafting table. Here, the hands unpack clear plastic rods from a plastic bag, which the speaker identifies as "balloon sticks from the Dollar Tree" (large size), placing them beside a white circular foam base, referred to as a "foam disc." The hands measure and cut the rods using a yellow tape measure, with the speaker mentioning the longest piece is "17 and a quarter inches," before spray-painting them with Rust-Oleum Metallic Gold spray paint, explicitly called "metallic gold spray paint" by the speaker, transforming them into shiny gold sticks. The speaker specifies using "10 pieces of those" balloon sticks and "24 pieces of my clear sticks" for the "wall sconces." The rods are then arranged and glued into the foam base, forming a decorative stand, with the final shot showing the completed gold stand ready for display.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a pair of hands, adorned with pink nails, meticulously measuring a clear plastic rod using a yellow tape measure. The crafter's voice explains, "So I need one piece of that," before detailing the precise measurements: "the next size down will be three quarters less, so that's 16 and a half inches," adding that "we're cutting four pieces of those." The hands then cut the rod with scissors, a process repeated for multiple clear rods which are laid out in parallel. The crafter continues, "each size, we're going to be cutting four pieces of each size and the next size will be 15 and three quarters and so on." She further specifies, "The next size after 15 and three quarters will be 15 inches and that's going to be two pieces of gold." Following this, a gold-painted plastic rod is measured and cut to match the clear ones, then arranged in a staggered pattern over them. The hands pick up a white circular foam base, which the crafter identifies as "my same foam disc," and begins inserting the gold rods into pre-drilled holes. She describes the arrangement: "we're using four clear, then separate it with one gold and we're doing the same for the opposite sides. Now this is our center piece." The crafter emphasizes finding "the center of my longest stick" and placing it "right on that foam and making sure that it's lined up properly," securing it with a hot glue gun, stating, "I'm using my hot glue for this." She advises, "Just make sure that first one is lined up properly and then you'll get all of them correct," as she carefully positions the rods for even spacing and alignment, all under the 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark.

Next, the hands measure and cut a gold-painted plastic rod to match the length of the clear rods. The gold rods are then arranged in a staggered pattern over the clear rods, creating a layered effect. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a top-down view of a crafting process, set to gentle, upbeat electronic music. The hands carefully arrange several blocks in a grid pattern, then apply Gorilla Wood Glue from its distinctive white bottle with a brown cap along the seams. The speaker instructs to "place that piece of foam that you cut off in the center, just make sure that it's centered," and then adds, "I'm using my wood glue to connect four more pieces of my tower blocks." The hands precisely squeeze the glue and press the blocks together to form a solid panel, with the speaker noting she's "turning the skinny side upwards." The methodical process continues, adding more blocks and aligning them, with the speaker emphasizing, "Making sure that it's lined up properly with the base and make sure that it's very close to the foam disk." The faint 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark is visible in the background, reinforcing the crafting channel's identity, while a blue box of wooden blocks, a yellow ruler, and a black cable are also present in the workspace.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a top-down view of a white work surface where a pair of hands with dark skin and manicured nails are seen painting small, irregular wooden pieces. A female narrator, whose voice accompanies the actions, explains she's "using this black tempera paint from the Dollar Tree" with a "dry brush" technique to make them look like "a dark copper," also painting the back and sides. She adds black paint to "lighter pieces" for a better transition, all while a gentle, upbeat instrumental background music plays. The text 'Budget Friendly Creations' is faintly visible as a watermark. After painting, the scene transitions to a rectangular black frame on the same white surface. The narrator states, "Now our frame is dry," as the hands arrange thin, gold-painted wooden dowels into a geometric pattern of intersecting triangles and lines. She refers to these as "skewers" and describes "placing my design in the center" and resting it "right on top of those skewers." The hands then meticulously use a tube of Gorilla Wood Glue to secure the joints of the dowels, with the narrator identifying it as "my Aleene's Mix Media adhesive," applying it precisely at each intersection to hold the structure together, culminating in a completed geometric framework.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a top-down view of a crafting process, set to light, instrumental background music. A pair of hands with dark skin and manicured nails meticulously assembles a geometric wall art piece within a black rectangular frame, which already features a network of gold-painted wooden dowels arranged in intersecting triangular and linear patterns on a white background. A female voice begins narrating, advising viewers that this is where they'll need to cut their "wooden pieces" *after* making their frame for easier assembly, explaining that she got lucky on most but cutting after is generally easier. The hands then position a small, triangular wooden piece into one of the open triangular spaces. The narrator continues, instructing to place the wooden pieces underneath the frame, use a pencil to draw the shape, and then cut it. As the hands apply white adhesive from a tube labeled 'Adhesive', the narrator identifies it as "my Alien's mixed media adhesive" (likely Aleene's), explaining that she's placing the pieces right in the center of each "skewer" (referring to the gold dowels) so they can show from the sides. The hands carefully place the glued triangle into the frame and use a small blue tool to press and secure it firmly. The process repeats as the hands pick up another triangular wooden piece, apply adhesive, and prepare to insert it into an adjacent space, continuing the meticulous assembly. Faintly visible in the background is the watermark 'Budget Friendly Creations'. The narrator then transitions to a call to action, encouraging viewers to support her channel by giving a thumbs-up, leaving a comment (even just one word), or sharing the video with one contact, expressing her appreciation as the background music continues.

segments.integrated_caption: The video begins with a top-down view of a geometric wall art piece being assembled within a black frame, as a female voice explains, "Allow it to dry and hang it on the wall with a screw or a nail." Hands with dark skin and manicured nails carefully position triangular wooden pieces into a gold-painted dowel structure, temporarily securing them with blue painter’s tape. The speaker then proudly states, "And here's how this project turned out, guys. I hope you like this one," as the scene transitions to the completed artwork mounted on a gray wall. A light, upbeat instrumental track plays throughout. The focus shifts to a crafting table, and the speaker announces, "Let's move on to our next project. We're making a wall sconce." She identifies the next materials, stating, "For this project, we are using two cylinder vases from the Dollar Tree." The hands apply a white adhesive, which the speaker identifies as "two permanent glue for this one," specifically "an instant glue" and "my Eileen's mixed media," to the bottom rims of the clear glass vases, pressing them together to form a double-layered structure. Next, a light-colored wooden oval, described by the speaker as "this wooden plank from the Dollar Tree," is shown. The hands apply adhesive to its edge, and the speaker explains, "I'm going to take it outside and spray paint it with my metallic gold spray paint," referring to the Rust-Oleum Metallic Gold spray paint. The video concludes with the hands holding the now shiny gold-painted oval, ready for the next step, with the 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark visible in the background.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a female voice stating, "Now it's dry, I'm going to attach my pieces together," as a pair of hands with dark skin and manicured nails applies a clear adhesive from a white tube labeled 'Jewelry & Metal Glue' to the edge of a light-colored wooden oval. The speaker clarifies, "I'm using the instant glue in the center... Also using my permanent glue on the edges. Just use a generous amount." A transparent plastic tube with a white interior rod is then placed over the oval, aligning with the adhesive line, with the speaker advising, "Make sure you're lining it up properly, so it's nice and straight." The hands then use orange-handled wire cutters, which the speaker refers to as "clippers," to trim the plastic tube to size, explaining, "And I'm using my clippers to keep it from rolling until it starts to set." Following this, a roll of gold metallic tape, identified by the speaker as "gold contact paper," is unrolled, revealing a grid-patterned backing paper. The speaker mentions, "I'm cutting seven rows of it," and "I'll place this contact paper in the center of my two vases." The tape, described as "self-adhesive" by the speaker, is cut to size with scissors and then carefully wrapped around the plastic tube, covering the adhesive area. The hands smooth the tape down, ensuring it adheres evenly, with the speaker noting, "this will keep the contact paper nice and straight." The final step involves trimming the excess tape with scissors, leaving a neat, gold-wrapped tube with the wooden oval securely attached, as the speaker instructs, "Just cut the excess off, measure it, make sure you have the right amount." Throughout the segment, the faint white watermark 'Budget Friendly Creations' is visible in the background on the clean, white work surface, under consistent, bright lighting.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a pair of hands, dark-skinned with manicured nails, meticulously crafting a decorative piece. A female voice instructs to "cut it in the line" as the hands position a transparent plastic tube over a wooden oval, partially wrapped in gold tape. The hands then use a hot glue gun, applying adhesive to the tube's edge, following the instruction to "use your hot glue to attach it." A strip of grid-patterned paper is pressed onto the glue, aligning precisely. The speaker explains an alternative, suggesting one "can also spray paint the back here, this white part, before you place it on the glass" so "it won't be showing through." However, she clarifies her chosen method: "what I'm going to be using to hide that white from showing through the edges, I'm going to use my rhinestone ribbon just to go around the two ends of my contact paper." The hands proceed to wrap the tube with gold tape, smoothing it to secure the paper. A strip of sparkling rhinestone trim, which the speaker identifies as "rhinestone ribbon" and notes is "self-adhesive," is then applied to the gold-wrapped section, with her adding, "I'm just placing one on each end." Finally, a piece of yellow fabric is wrapped around the base of the tube and adjusted for a neat finish. Tools like scissors and a utility knife are visible on the white work surface, and the 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark remains faintly in the background. The speaker concludes by describing a future step for the finished item, stating, "To hang our wall sconce on the wall, I'm using this picture frame hook with the two screws that came with them. And I'm just screwing them onto..." The overall atmosphere is clean and focused on precise decorative detail.

segments.integrated_caption: Against a backdrop faintly marked with the 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark, a pair of hands with dark skin and neatly manicured nails meticulously arranges four square photo frames, featuring elegant gold borders and black inner frames, into a precise 2x2 grid on a white surface. Gentle, upbeat background music accompanies the scene as small wooden blocks, taken from a plastic bag, are used as spacers to connect and stabilize the frames. A female narrator guides the process, instructing to "Make sure that the frames are lined up properly" to achieve a "perfect cross in the center." The hands then place a fifth frame centrally, overlapping the initial four, with the narrator advising to "make sure that you're seeing even amount of the space between the four corners." Following the narrator's instruction to "use your pencil to mark where you place the frame," a yellow pencil with a red eraser is used to precisely mark the positions on the central frame where the wooden blocks will be inserted for stability. The narrator further clarifies to "mark the frame on the top and the one on the base." After carefully removing the central frame as instructed, the narrator reveals the adhesive being used: "I'm going to be using Aleene's Mixed Media Adhesive for this project." A white tube of this adhesive, labeled 'Jewelry & Metal Glue', is then applied along the inner edges of the central photo frame, preparing it for attachment to the underlying structure, with the narrator directing to "Place the glue where you mark the frames." The actions throughout are precise and deliberate, focusing on alignment and preparation for gluing the frames into a cohesive decorative piece.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment features a crafting process set to calm, instrumental background music, where a pair of hands with dark skin and manicured nails, one adorned with a gold ring, meticulously assembles a wall art piece. Initially, four square photo frames with gold borders and black inner frames are arranged in a 2x2 grid on a white surface. A female voice instructs, "Now be very careful and put the frame back in place." The hands then apply 'Jewelry & Metal Glue' from a white tube to the inner edges of a fifth frame, which is centrally positioned over the grid, overlapping the four frames to create a geometric design. The speaker explains, "I allowed mine to dry and then I flipped it over. And I'm going to be using four of my little wooden blocks and place it right in between those spaces, and this will keep our frame nice and strong." Following this, a black and yellow hot glue gun is used to attach the small wooden blocks between the frames, with the voice clarifying, "I'm using hot glue first, and then I'll use my strong adhesive for a more permanent bond." The frames are later painted with a metallic silver finish, transforming their appearance, as the hands continue to apply adhesive to the wooden blocks, pressing them into place for stability. The speaker then directly addresses the audience, requesting, "Please remember to give me a thumbs up before you leave, it helps to support my channel. Also, please leave me a comment, just one word is good enough. Thank you." The methodical process, characterized by precise movements and attention to alignment, culminates in a cohesive, layered wall art piece. The voice concludes by detailing the next step: "To hook our wall decoration on the wall, I'm using a picture frame hook and I'm using my strong adhesive to attach my hook, and I'm placing it right behind that space where no one can see it." A faint 'Budget Friendly Creations' watermark is visible throughout the segment.
